card.intuition: An attack that tricks a logged-in user into submitting a malicious request to a web application where they are authenticated.
card.analogy: Forging a signature on a check: if a bank (web app) trusts a signature (session cookie) regardless of who actually handed the check to the teller, an attacker can trick you into 'signing' a transfer by hiding the check inside a 'Free Kittens!' flyer you clicked on.
when_to_use: Securing state-changing operations (Delete Account, Transfer Money, Change Password)
Protecting applications that use Cookie-based session authentication
gotchas: CSRF only works if the user is ALREADY logged in to the target site
APIs that use Bearer tokens (JWT) in the Authorization header are generally immune to CSRF because browsers don't send that header automatically (unlike cookies)

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
Attacker crafts a request that triggers a state-changing action.
Browser auto-attaches session cookies to cross-site requests.
Server must verify CSRF token in request body or custom header.
SameSite=Strict cookies and Origin validation block the attack.

exercise.prompt: Identify the standard defense against CSRF that involves adding a unique, secret value to ogni non-GET request.
hints: It matches the acronym of the attack
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.lower() == "csrf"
assert check("csrf")
print("PASS")
